original_abstract:
This paper explores the application of single-layer and deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for the analysis of remote sensing data. Applying supervised convolutional networks to multi- and hyper-spectral imagery is notoriously difficult due to the high dimensionality of the input data and the chronic shortage of labeled samples. To address this, we propose a greedy, layer-wise unsupervised pre-training strategy integrated with a highly efficient algorithm designed for the unsupervised learning of sparse features. This algorithm, rooted in sparse representation theory, simultaneously enforces both population and lifetime sparsity to ensure the extraction of high-quality features.

We demonstrate the expressive power of these learned representations across several diverse remote sensing scenarios: aerial scene classification, land-use mapping in very high resolution (VHR) imagery, and land-cover classification using multispectral and hyperspectral data. The proposed method significantly outperforms standard baseline techniques, such as Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and kernel PCA (kPCA), as well as current state-of-the-art aerial classification algorithms, all while maintaining high computational efficiency during the training phase. Our findings indicate that while single-layer convolutional networks can extract effective discriminative features—provided their receptive fields account for neighboring pixels—they are best suited for tasks requiring high-resolution, detailed outputs. In contrast, deep architectures prove far superior, successfully capturing increasing levels of abstraction and structural complexity through their hierarchical feature layers.
humanized_abstract:
Deep features learning for remote sensing using deep/ shallow CNNs Most of the current works on remote sensing data analysis using single-layer and deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs) perform analysis in the supervised fashion by applying the learned models directly to multi- and hyper-spectral remote sensing data. However, the high dimensionality of remote sensing data as well as the scarcity of labeled training samples make it challenging to directly apply the learned models and make use of remote sensing data. In this paper, we develop a greedy, layer-wise unsupervised pre-training approach along with an efficient unsupervised learning algorithm for sparse feature learning. We leverage the power of sparse representation to propose a powerful algorithm that not only enforces population sparsity, but also limits the lifetime sparsity.

Learned features are shown to be applicable to a number of distant remote sensing applications including aerial scene classification, land-use classification from very high resolution (VHR) images, and land-cover classification from multispectral and hyperspectral data. Our results show a significant improvement in accuracy over a number of conventional and advanced techniques, including PCA and kernel PCA (kPCA), compared to the state-of-the-art in aerial classification techniques, and in addition achieve a computational advantage during network training. It is argued that single-layer convolutional networks, when receptive fields are limited to neighbourhood pixels, can indeed learn good discriminative feature descriptions that are well-suited for applications where detailed, fine-scale outputs are required. On the other hand, deep networks can learn higher-level representations with increasing abstractness and complexity, utilizing their hierarchical structures.

We leverage the power of sparse representation to propose a powerful algorithm that not only enforces population sparsity, but also limits the lifetime sparsity.

Learned features are shown to be applicable to a number of distant remote sensing applications including aerial scene classification, land-use classification from very high resolution (VHR) images, and land-cover classification from multispectral and hyperspectral data. document.input:
This paper explores the application of single-layer and deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for the analysis of remote sensing data. Applying supervised convolutional networks to multi- and hyper-spectral imagery is notoriously difficult due to the high dimensionality of the input data and the chronic shortage of labeled samples. To address this, we propose a greedy, layer-wise unsupervised pre-training strategy integrated with a highly efficient algorithm designed for the unsupervised learning of sparse features. This algorithm, rooted in sparse representation theory, simultaneously enforces both population and lifetime sparsity to ensure the extraction of high-quality features.

We demonstrate the expressive power of these learned representations across several diverse remote sensing scenarios: aerial scene classification, land-use mapping in very high resolution (VHR) imagery, and land-cover classification using multispectral and hyperspectral data. The proposed method significantly outperforms standard baseline techniques, such as Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and kernel PCA (kPCA), as well as current state-of-the-art aerial classification algorithms, all while maintaining high computational efficiency during the training phase. Our findings indicate that while single-layer convolutional networks can extract effective discriminative features—provided their receptive fields account for neighboring pixels—they are best suited for tasks requiring high-resolution, detailed outputs. In contrast, deep architectures prove far superior, successfully capturing increasing levels of abstraction and structural complexity through their hierarchical feature layers.
